scenarios.scenario: You're working on a project to breaking down a large e-commerce monolithic client server application into a set of microservices so it will scale better.  The architecture specs include a lot of technology that is new to the team (new frontend and backend frameworks, adding in API gateway and messaging queues.) Currently, each team is working to build a single micro-service.  The VP of IT is wanting assurances that the new tech is going to work. How can you do that?
insights: Build teh smalll UI then implement the AP to suporting and the DB to suppor that and stand it up with all the tech compontients.
Do an end to end demo that uses all the components
Touch as few components as possible at first
Start with everything returning default success
Define interfaces for that path
Pick a full path. Maybe view catalog
Add more components as things progress

scenarios.scenario:
You are hired to lead the migration of a legacy investment platform to the cloud before the next reporting cycle in 4 months. The system is a maze of low-code dashboards, Python pipelines, APIs, reporting tools, spreadsheets, and manual steps. Documentation is thin. Many of the people who understood the system are gone.
Architects want the target architecture first. Data teams want the full model defined upfront. Business teams want every workflow mapped before delivery starts. Everyone is making progress. Nobody understands the whole system.
What is the smallest slice we could deliver in 6 weeks that exercises every critical layer end-to-end?
insights: Get at least one representative of each part of the system together in one room. Create connections for communications between the teams and address potential blockers (resentment, shyness, fear of overcommitting to technical decisions, etc)
Ask each team to make a list of their dependencies/blockers, from most crucial to least crucial. Get at the WHY of why they feel they can't start before they have that thing settled.
Ask each team to draw a map of the whole system, however incomplete/incorrect that map may be. Meet to discuss the maps and come up with a shared one.
Emphasize that we need to focus on an Minimum Viable Product. Proof of concept, not delve into every aspect the system will eventually support.
focus on input-output
downside - culture
make the implicit explicit
bring a representative of each team and discuss way to work and blindspots
Maybe the first delivery should be logging in and retrieving a customer profile.
What if the first step was teaching everyone about how the cloud changes thing?

1. Identify the primary business function of the system--who cares about its outputs?


2. Identify the most essential of those outputs--what inputs are used to create those outputs?


3. Create a thin, end-to-end version of the system that transforms those inputs into the minimum desirable outputs.

scenarios.scenario: Since the team started using AI agents (Claude, Cursor, Windsurf, etc.), your "Velocity" has increased by 500%. On paper, you are the most productive team in the company. However, the some developers have a "nagging feeling" that the codebase is becoming a "haunted forest" of generated code that no one fully understands. Management wants to use this new "High Velocity" as the new baseline for all future performance reviews.
insights: Short term productivity gains cannot be replicated in every team or every project
WHEN something breaks, we are far less certain how to fix it.
We don't agree on the need to clean code as we go.
Speed never was the bottleneck when it came to coding - understanding was and still is.
Mgmt doesn't understand the impact of MORE code on productivity., perforamcna or ultimate outcomes.
have them explain what they mean by "productivity gain"
What areas of the codebase are unknown to people?
The resulting fear / unsecurity / lack of trust in quality might sink the ship.
Can everyone explain the system and its pieces at a high level?
What is productivity?
People can estimate higher complexities
Lack of feedback loop with quality
Productivity gains are not measuring full picture
I'm a domain person. My job relies on me knowing all the things
Creates wrong incentives
PEOPLE
Earlier control was important to management, now loss of control might result.
Agents create more code than we can keep up with reviewing, leaving a scary traill,
Communication between management and engineering can be difficult
Second-order effects
We haven't figured out the role of testing (all kinds) when working with agents.
Fear of layoffs
No agreement on the governance model/constitution for working with agents.
OPTIMISING FOR METRICS
the spec doesn't include changes that happen along the way
What happens in future if the "haunted forest" reanimates?
Future government restrictions on AI intelligence
Taking the time to understand and clarify the codebase is seen as unproductive
Are there noticeable areas of growing tech debt or bugs?
We might reach a point where any change breaks something else
Security - what vulnerabilities do we introduce through AI Agents?
Cost - at some point the cost of AI could go up.
The pain of unexpected consequences.
We might start doing no-value work to reach the targets
Debt
Is codebase diverging from original/expected design?
What if the company gets restricted on using the most up-to-date models

scenarios.scenario:
You work for a SaaS company with a popular but aging monolithic platform that customers depend on every day. It is now slow to
 change, hard to scale, and full of behavior no one on the team fully  understands. Leadership wants a path to a more reliable
  platform, but customers cannot lose critical workflows and the current system still has to keep running. Your team needs to

scenarios.scenario:
The board has told you to modernise your IBM mainframe Architecture within the next 6 months.  Your team only has 3 developers with the required domain knowledge and depth of skill and language. 
At the same time the business is implementing 3 new products that this team also needs to support. 
Your 3 developers are taking shortcuts in their planning and execution of the modernisation and the new product implementation in order to keep up with the demand.

What would you do if you had enough.
insights: an abundance of Risk
Pair program everything to share knowledge and make progress
Creat an Abundance of Focus for the 3 developers
hire a bunch of IBM consultants and tell them to sort out the modernisation
make 2 teams so that the developers could only focus on 1 thing at a time
Can AI tools augment our work to make things better
Get the 3 devs to only focus on the modernisation
Hire more engineers
An abundance of freedom to experiment
buy millions of claude credits and get claude to modernise and rearchitect the whole mainframe
Abundance of time, can we defer new products, which is slowing us down more
an abundance of API's
Abundance of push back, what can we stop doing
stop doing one of the things
pair youngsters with more experienced people
put juniors on the rearchitecture project with a product manager as a pair
Put new developers on new projects and keep domain knowledge
Take the time for longer-term planning to ensure trade-offs are intentional and not accidental
Identify the project with the lowest barrier to entry and train additional people on that
Push off one or more projects to focus on the most important elements first
Take the time getting other developers up to speed on the needed skills and knowledge to get this done
Take the time needed to complete the project
Hire consultants with the right skills to focus on certain projects

Your team needs to look for options beyond dividing too little time between two important commitments.

Give V1 customers V2 for free forever.

scenarios.scenario: Your team has been running two auth systems in parallel for eight months. Everyone agrees the legacy system needs to go — it's causing duplicated bugs, onboarding confusion, and double the maintenance. The new system handles about 70% of use cases. The remaining 30% are a mix of edge cases, integrations with other teams' systems, and a couple of things nobody fully understands. Every sprint, migration work loses priority to feature delivery. There's no disagreement about whether to cut over. There's just no date. This morning's retro raised it again. Someone said "we really need to sort out the auth migration." Everyone nodded. The conversation moved on.
insights: Can someone take the work as priority?
Schedule a mobbing/barn-raising day
No new feature work until a date is decided.
"No-one is allowed to get a coffee until we have a date."
What if we switched off the old system today?
The license for the legacy system expires June 30. Figure it out.
How much time do we lose per sprint by supporting both systems?
If this is a prioritiy why isn't it a prioritiy?
What is needed so we actually understand the remaining needed work
Who want's to champion this and set a date for done?
Is there a reason we don't want to do this work?

scenarios.scenario:
You work for an e-commerce site that produces customisable T-Shirts, their tagline is “Your shirt, your way”.

The last three months were spent delivering the company’s first AI driven product: “Super You” that turns you (or a loved one) into a superhero via a couple of prompts and an image upload.
The Monday morning after the release, the team gather and the product manager is looking a little sheepish.

"So congrats on getting “Super You” out last week. The board love it.” they say, “Now I guess you’re curious about what we’re going to do next. Well…with all the excitement of the last few months, discovery kind of went out of the window and..and we’ve not got anything on the backlog.”
“Oh yes, and our designer is off on holiday for two weeks and the commercial team are working on the ad campaign for the new shirts.”


The PM has several possible things the team could work on, in various stages of discovery. Some have some rough UX design sketches, some are nothing more than a sentence scribbled down on a post-it a few weeks ago. None are what the team might call “ready”.

The Engineering Lead has some tech debt incurred during the recent build that the engineers really want to address and a few tech “initiatives” that they’d love to get their teeth stuck into. 

The PM and Engineering Lead start to discuss priorities and are going round in circles, unclear of the cost or impact of anything they have brought to the table. You watch the time tick away...
insights: tackle debt incurred from recent feature
Ask PM to choose the work item with the highest value
Does this lead to fun/easy bit thinkies
Don't commit to a large changes
Have eng team vote on highest value tech work
Ask if team will commit to N weeks/sprints of pure tech debt work
time box - 1 week?
Look into the previous outages cause by tech debt to pick one.
Flip the coin
What can we do in the given time constraint?

scenarios.scenario: Your new manager just dropped a 400-ticket backlog into your lap — bugs, half-finished features, ancient refactors, and one ticket from 2022 simply titled "fix the thing." Sprint planning is in two weeks and you're expected to walk in with a plan. You open the list, your eyes glaze over, and you find yourself reorganizing your desk instead. Where do you start?
insights: Take a bug that seems not too hard and write a lot of tests around it.
Implement a simple ticket. Two persons, the same ticket, and then compare.
Find the refactoring tickets. Those teach you a lot about the code.
Use AI to analyze the backlog and find any common themese
Randomly pick 10 tickets from the backlog and spend no more than 10 minutes on each to get a feel for what you have to tackle
Find tickets that are close to your competence
Find the most simple bug that can be fixed and do that
Find any ticket that makes sense
sort tickets by complete / incomplete
take complete tickets and sort by urgency
Determine what is the priority of the sprint
Find obsolete tickets and close them out

scenarios.scenario:
You're the new engineering lead on an on-prem server VM platform team.
Your customers, App teams, hate it: to spin up a VM takes two hours of config options. The platform CLI for managing the VMs is too limited. Missing QoL features, like a status UI.
Execs are upset adoption has stalled.
Your team is demoralised. The backlog is huge.They all have different theories on what to fix first: streamline provisioning, extend the CLI, add a UI, run developer enablement sessions, and just "add AI". Where do you start?`
insights: Status widget  bake-off - pick winners to add to a  gallery of options
do we have some user feedback? what wishes or problems are most mentioned? -> they're all probably fun in some way, but we should do the most imnpactful
Spend a day in the customer's shoes (shoulder surfing)
Gamify the Onboarding. Turn it into a quest
Use AI to generate common and unique automation for deployments an scalling
play "user" - each of us temporarily goes working in an App team to see what are the worst problems
Introduce Feature Flags
IF no user feedback, add "user feedback" feature - pop in "Give feedback" widgets all over (or at least in the tricky sections) UI/CLI
Use the competing external products the app teams like
have AI prototype some UX improvements
Host app team hackathon focused on using CLI
Analyse usage logs
Archive the whole backlog

scenarios.scenario:
You work for a SaaS company that provides online shops for local shoe stores across Europe. Due to popular demand, Bitcoin is being added as a payment method — but all prices are currently displayed in Euros, both in the apps and websites. Prices come from the product API as a numeric value and are rendered in the UI with formatting and a hardcoded Euro symbol.


For example, a shoe with price € 40.87 will need to display ₿ 0.00058 if the user selected BTC as preferred currency.
insights: Sell all shoes for the same price
Invent a new currency as the base
Are the same questions applicable to mass/weight? Dimensions? What else?
How many currencies would a shopper like to see?
Could monetory units and formatting be a shopper preference?
"money" as a general concept: formatting, symbol, precision, ...
Think about Abundance
Choose a base currency and allow other currencies by a conversion service

Make it work with different currencies first. Then add bitcoin as a kind of currency.
Also an example of "make the change easy"
How do we handle a variable amount of currencies?
give products away - initiating a non-monetary economy
start a global initiative to switch to Euro in both classic & crypto money systems
allow modifiable user currency
replace prices with fuzzy stuff like "CHEAP!" or "fancy shmancy"
Add functionality for any cryptocurrency
Handle any currency
Sometimes problems can also be too general?
Question requirements - do we need to display BTC on listing pages?
Changing specificity = reframing
Changed assumption = need to generalise or specialise
convert to trading goods (vegetables, etc.) instead of money
Shoecoin

scenarios.scenario: You're working on an organizational effort of breaking down a large monolithic service into a set of microservices. During this transition, you receive a bug report involving multiple services, but as you investigate, you realize there’s no way to correlate log messages across services.

You've been working on a product. The company decided to consolidate its tech stack, and your Ruby app needs to be migrated to Python. The team is 3 developers: 1 senior, 1 mid, 1 junior. The PM and EM want maximum capacity on the migration. They'll push back to slow client work, but can't give you full-time focus. Bug fixes and feature requests keep coming. New features can be built in Python, but code dependencies don't always split cleanly. You're staring at this Monday morning. The whole thing feels enormous. Where do you even start?
insights: Start identifying an isolated flow(s) that we can start abstracting with a wrapper that calls the python path
Hi!
Let the junior dev pick work item first
Start with writing a small unit test for business logic
Make a "hello world" app
Easiest thing = small
Build a little skeleton in Python that does nothing but we can deploy it with one button
Make it easy to do easy things , so work should be chunkable
Cluster features by size and start from a small one
Deployable version of the smallest peice of that - E2E

scenarios.scenario:
You are a knowledge worker who is watching the growing excitement around AI with both interest and concern. Interest, because you see indications of great potential; concern, because there are so many tools and opinions that it is hard to comprehend it all. You also don't want to be "left behind" as the opportunities grow.
What is something specific that you notice that you are doing every day (or at least multiple times in a week) that you could try using AI to advise, plan, or even execute?
insights: Flagging emails that haven't received a response
Helping me fine tune my resume and cover letter
Researching job opportunities
Reading through my emails to pull out action items, important emails, etc.
Reformatting long, rambling emails or presentations into quick bullet lists or tables
Collect emails on a specific project and summarize to let me know what's happening and what questions are coming up.
diving through my growing todo list
suggesting a jumping off point for tasks that are confusing
identify potential security risks in a system design
Giving me summary of what happened yesterday in all the e-mails, messages, and meetings I was part of. So that I have an quick overview, where to follow-up and what TODOs I have.
PR reviews
Try to connect a new tool (slack, Google drive, datadog, etc.) each day, see what insights the AI can come up with
tracking down answers to questions that have already been asked
following up on all the things I'm waiting on from other people
Keeping track of what's to do/in progress/done across the full team
Try one small side quest with AI each week (e.g. a small tidy, making a test faster, adding a rubocop rule, etc.)
Weekly status updates to share up and out
organizing my enormous pile of utility scripts
Managing my calendar

scenarios.scenario:
You are an Engineering Manager, responsible for the productivity of your team. 


Your organization is rolling out AI coding tools and leadership says they want everyone using a "Dark Factory" tool by the end of the next quarter: no code is read or written by a human. A few engineers are enthusiastic about this, but most are scared and lacking in skills and experience, some mourning the loss of their identity as software craftspeople, reluctant to even get started.

This goal feels overly ambitious given the human constraints. What will you do?

build isolation and containerization.
Have half the write/read using the tool? (Ex: just the "write" part)
Mob for the first 6 weeks to build the first framework together, equalising the levels of the team
Have 50% of people in each team using it, and 50% suffering to fix all the slop
Pick one small piece of code and build the factory pipelines until the code quality is maximised.
send half the team on sabatical
delete half the code

recharge batteries

scenarios.scenario:
A new feature needs to touch a messy part of the codebase. The team knows the area is fragile. Tests are weak. Names are misleading. Responsibilities are scattered.


One engineer wants to refactor first. Another says the business cannot wait and the feature should go in first, with cleanup afterwards.
Refactoring first could make the change safer, but it could also expand scope before value is delivered. Shipping first could protect time to market, but it may deepen the mess and make the next change worse.


The feature is valuable, but not urgent enough to justify reckless work. The code is bad, but not bad enough to justify an open-ended cleanup.
Do you refactor first, ship first, or implement one vertical slice both ways: one tiny behaviour change after tidying and one without, then compare the real cost of change?
insights: How little can we do?
Think smaller
Refactor "just enough"
Create a "fork" in the code itself (similar to a strangler pattern).
Take smaller bites.
Create two branches, one for the feature another for refactor.
Clear definition of done for small step
Timebox both for 1-2 days as experiment
Don't allow scope creep
Make sure to write the tests. If all else fails, the tests at least will be in place.
can an isolated refactor make one part of the feature easier to deliver or is the refactor a big bang?
Start work bit by bit and do the refactoring for that bit if necessary.
Explicit requirements for testing when doing feature development
Make the change easy, make the change
Flat out refuse to do either.
Do both, but delay one.
Is there anything to refactor with the highest reward? Do this first
Do AI-led refactoring in parallel with feature work

In the age of AI - run one agent to implement without refactor. 
It will ideally help to see the messiness if there is one
Both people pair
Combine with half-in-half
Switch feature and refactor in 2 hour increments

scenarios.scenario: You manage the roadmap for a product where a subset of customers consistently struggle with the time allocation features--confusing calculations and slow loading times. Customer success has flagged it for three quarters, but each time it gets bumped for new feature work. Each time you review the metrics with the engineering and product leadership, they categorize the impact as "within acceptable SLA thresholds." Most of the affected customers are on legacy plans and lack strong executive sponsors. You're starting to suspect the people setting those SLA thresholds would set them differently if they were the ones reading the support tickets.

scenarios.scenario:
Management at your company is sold on AI coding agents after using them for small tasks and prototypes. Now they want Engineering to finish, by the end of the quarter, a major platform restructuring originally estimated at one year.


Engineering uses AI too, but the team is alarmed. Leadership is extrapolating from low-risk tasks to high-risk system work, and the deadline is already forcing rushed decisions. 
Concerns have been raised about what might break, who will carry the burden, and whether speed is being mistaken for progress. Leadership keeps asking for more speed.


How could the team “put skin in the game” so decision-makers directly experience the risks and trade-offs of this AI-agent strategy before scaling it across the entire company?
insights: Ask for an estimation of available token budget for the task and approve it.
Get management to use AI to forecast the investments needed to speed up high risk system work, and decide to spend the amount
Have everyone share an example of how AI didn't execute well on a task or project they have expertise in
Propose to use AI agents to do other important work in the company - e.g. accounting.
Ask the managers to create a plan using AI agents and commit to that plan.
Have everyone try AI on a Difficult task and share where the AI gaps are
Have the decision makers choose a difficult task to use AI on and share their findings.

scenarios.scenario: Your team is modernizing a legacy application. In a planning meeting, a key stakeholder says, “Obviously, we need Feature X integrated into the new platform for the initial launch, as it's critical for our biggest client." What do you do?
insights: Obiviously, it would be important to conduct a new customer discovery process to confirm priorities before proceeding.
Obviously we should not replicate the same behavior in the new version, so we need to do some research first
Obiviously, what evidence do we have to proceed with this feature X?
Question whether it would also be a must for the 2nd biggest client. Observe chaos ensuing. :)
What if we delay it for after the launch? What would happen?
Obviously we should prioritize features on total usage across All clients
Obviously we should keep feature X in the legacy application until after the launch. It's too important to risk breaking.
Do we really need it ? Let's ask the client how important that feature is by using a quick rating, and dig further if needed
let's discuss all the priorities and then we can look at how the features fit together
Obviously we could launch without it and find a simpler way to achieve the same outcome
Give a feature Y as an alternative and check whether we have 2 prio 1

scenarios.scenario:
"Obviously using AI to increase output is what our clients want." You work for a consulting firm that largely supplies software engineering staff augmentation.


Make the opposite assumption. Think about what may not be so obvious. What are the assumptions in the statement? Come up with 5 or more thoughts (stickies).
insights: client doesn't understand AI; is afraid
What if our clients don't know how they want to use AI?
What if "increased output" isn't the ultimate goal?
What if our clients want to use AI to grow their staff
The hype is strong in AI - what if it is too early for going "all in"?
what if clients care about outcomes and output is a distraction
what if our clients don't want to use AI at all
People might be overwhelmed by too much output.
What about downstream processes? Can they absorb more output?
heavily regulated, must read every line of code
increased output moves the bottleneck - who will make sense of it all?
Oviously our clients don't want us using AI, they're paying for engineering.
what if our clients want more considered outcomes not more output
What if our clients want to use AI to help them discern what to build instead of just building everything that no one wants
What if clients actually don't want more outcomes not more output
Cognition is a scarce resource. Too much output might be problematic.
What if our clients want AI to help them reduce complexity
